source: Graham & Doddsville (Columbia Business School) — Investor interview with Gavin Baker, CIO of Atreides Management (Issue 43, Fall 2021)
summary: 5G is not a meaningful enabler of new use cases and represents a cost treadmill for incumbents
summary_extended: Baker argues that 5G is fundamentally different from prior generational wireless upgrades in that it enables no new 'killer application' — unlike 3G, which enabled the mobile internet and app ecosystems, and 4G, which enabled streaming services like Spotify and Netflix. He states flatly that '5G changes nothing at all,' characterizing it as a cost treadmill rather than a revenue opportunity: carriers must spend heavily on spectrum and infrastructure simply to maintain competitive position, not to unlock new monetizable use cases. At best, 5G marginally enables IoT deployments and allows mobile operators to compete at the margin with fixed-line broadband providers, neither of which represents a transformative revenue driver. The investment implication is structurally bearish on incumbent mobile operators, who face sustained elevated capex without a corresponding uplift in ARPU or addressable market, compressing long-term returns on invested capital.
detail: Baker argues 5G changes nothing fundamentally. Unlike 3G (enabled mobile internet/apps) and 4G (enabled streaming/Spotify/Netflix), 5G does not enable any new killer application. At best it marginally enables IoT and allows mobile operators to compete with fixed-line providers on the margin. Incumbents must spend heavily just to maintain position.
confidence_evidence: Baker states flatly 'the short answer is 5G changes nothing at all' with no hedging; frames it as a structural view not a tentative one

ticker_audit.reasoning: AT&T is a major US incumbent mobile operator that must spend heavily on 5G capex with no new killer app to drive incremental revenue — directly subject to the cost treadmill thesis.
Verizon is a major US incumbent mobile operator facing the same structural capex burden with no meaningful new revenue enablement from 5G — directly subject to this thesis.

T-Mobile is a US mobile operator directly subject to the 5G cost treadmill dynamic, though its competitive position as a challenger may differ; still a direct subject of the thesis about 5G not enabling new use cases.

Orange SA is a European telecom operator. While thematically related, Baker's discussion appears focused on the US market context and ORAN is not a US-listed primary equity — it trades as an ADR but was not specifically named; the thesis implications are sufficiently captured by T, VZ, and TMUS without adding a loosely implied foreign operator.
